text:
A color I keep coming back to is a deep teal, the kind that sits right at the seam between blue and green where you can't quite say which one is winning. It gets passed over a lot. People reach for navy when they want something serious or a brighter blue when they want something friendly, and teal ends up feeling like a compromise nobody chose on purpose. But I think that in-between quality is exactly what makes it good. It has the calm of blue without the coldness, and the life of green without tipping into something that feels too lush or sweet.

What I associate with it is mostly water that has some depth to it. Not a tropical postcard turquoise, but the color of a lake in late afternoon, or the deep end of a pool when the light is going, or old copper that has weathered into a soft verdigris on a roof or a statue. There's a settled, slightly aged feeling to it, like something that has been around long enough to stop trying to impress you. It reads as quietly confident rather than loud. I also tend to associate it with a certain kind of evening quiet, the moment after sunset when the sky still holds light but everything has cooled down a notch, and the world feels paused rather than ended.

If I had to name why it's underrated, I'd say it's because it rewards attention. A pure red or yellow announces itself instantly, but teal asks you to look a second longer to figure out what you're actually seeing, and that little pause is its own small pleasure.
